fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a Python library that handles URL/resource reference stuff in a structured way. Basically devs are constantly writing the same brittle string-splitting code to pull apart URLs and it keeps breaking in weird edge cases — especially around passwords leaking into logs, internationalized hostnames, and percent-encoded stuff not being handled consistently. We want something clean that parses a URL into its pieces, lets you navigate around (like going up a directory, resolving relative links), manipulate query params without losing ordering or those weird valueless flag params, and convert between the human-readable Unicode form and the ASCII-safe form. There should also be a way to register custom schemes with their own default ports and authority rules, similar to how we handled custom protocol metadata in that config-registry module from the auth service refactor.

Also important: when something is broken/malformed, it should NOT bubble up a raw Python exception with internal stack details — it needs to output a friendly category name so our API consumers aren't seeing implementation noise. Same deal for the password redaction thing; make sure that's handled consistently across all the output paths. We also want structural equality comparison for URLs so caching layers can deduplicate properly. The output format for all of these operations needs to be consistent and machine-readable. Please align with however we did the encoded vs decoded representation split last time.

trigger_keywords: password redaction format
how should userinfo be hidden
text_without_password output rule
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When rendering text_without_password, if the userinfo contains a colon (user:pass), keep the username and colon but replace everything after the colon with an empty string, producing e.g. 'user:@host'. If there is no password component, text_without_password equals text exactly. This applies to all output paths: encoded, decoded, and facade.

trigger_keywords: userinfo format encoded vs decoded
how is userinfo printed in encoded mode
userinfo output difference between modes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In encoded mode, userinfo is printed as a single quoted string: userinfo="user:pass". In decoded mode, if an authority is present, userinfo is printed as a JSON array of decoded components: userinfo=["user","password"]. If no authority is present but the reference is decoded, print userinfo=[""] (array with one empty string). The distinction is critical and must not be conflated.

trigger_keywords: default port for http https
what port number to show when no port in URL
effective port value when port is absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a URL has no explicit port, the effective port is derived from the scheme: http=80, https=443. This value is printed as port=80 or port=443 (an integer, not null). For decoded mode with no authority, port=null is printed. For custom registered schemes, the registered default port is used the same way.

trigger_keywords: error output format for malformed URLs
what to print when URL is invalid
neutral error category names list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Malformed input must never surface Python exception class names or tracebacks. The allowed neutral error category strings are: error=invalid_port (non-numeric port), error=invalid_url (bad authority, unclosed bracket, etc.), error=invalid_text_encoding (undecodable percent escape). Each error output must be followed by url=<original_input> on the next line. For scheme registration errors, print error=invalid_scheme_registration followed by scheme=<name>.

trigger_keywords: query pair ordering with duplicate keys
how are valueless query params represented
null value in query array meaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Query parameters are represented as an ordered list of [name, value] pairs. A valueless parameter (flag with no '=') has its value represented as JSON null in the query array output, e.g. ["zut",null]. Order must be preserved across all operations including add, set, and remove. 'set' replaces ALL pairs with the matching name with a single new pair at the position of the first match.

trigger_keywords: path segment escaping on append
what characters get escaped when appending a segment
how slash inside a segment name is encoded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When appending a literal path segment, any '/' characters embedded within the segment text must be percent-encoded as %2F. This prevents them from being interpreted as path separators. The resulting segment appears in the path array and rendered text with %2F, not as a real separator.

trigger_keywords: replace last segment trailing slash behavior
path_replace_last when URL ends with slash
sibling segment with directory-style path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For path_replace_last: if the path ends with an empty trailing segment (i.e. URL ends in '/'), the replacement segment is appended as a new child after the directory, so the result has the trailing segment replaced with the new name and no trailing slash. If the path does NOT end with '/', the last non-empty segment is replaced. In both cases the output path should not have a trailing empty segment.

trigger_keywords: decoded query update encoding behavior
how are unicode query names serialized back
percent encoding for decoded query set operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In decoded_query_update, query parameter names and values are provided as decoded Unicode strings. When serialized back to the URL text, they must be percent-encoded appropriately (e.g. space becomes %20). The decoded query array printed still shows the decoded form (e.g. [" ","space"]). A 'set' on decoded names must match all percent-encoded variants that decode to the same string, replacing them all with the single new encoded pair.

trigger_keywords: URI to IRI conversion rules
which characters stay encoded in IRI form
convert_reference_text target iri vs uri
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When converting to URI form (target=uri): encode all non-ASCII characters in path, query, fragment, and host (use punycode for host). When converting to IRI form (target=iri): decode percent-encoded sequences that represent valid Unicode non-ASCII characters back to their literal Unicode form; decode punycode hostnames back to Unicode. The printed output includes host, path array, query array, and fragment in the converted representation.

trigger_keywords: normalize reference what operations are performed
normalization steps for scheme and host
dot segment removal during normalization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Normalization performs: (1) lowercase scheme and host, (2) remove default port for the scheme, (3) resolve dot segments (. and ..) in the path, (4) decode percent-encoded octets that are in the unreserved character set (letters, digits, '-', '.', '_', '~') — e.g. %7E becomes ~, %41 becomes A. The output prints scheme, host, path array, and query array.

trigger_keywords: register scheme with default port and authority flag
invalid scheme registration conditions
what makes a scheme registration invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A scheme registration is invalid if: (1) uses_authority=false but a default_port is provided (path-only schemes cannot have ports), or (2) uses_authority is null/not a boolean (must be explicitly true or false). Valid registrations with uses_authority=true and a default_port will suppress that port from rendered text and use it as the effective port value.

trigger_keywords: URL equality comparison and hashing
how are two URLs compared for identity
distinct_hash_count meaning in compare_urls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: URL comparison uses normalized structural identity: two references are equal if their parsed and normalized components are identical. The output prints equal=true/false, distinct_hash_count (the number of unique hash values in a set containing both — 1 if equal, 2 if distinct), and first_text/second_text showing each URL's rendered string form.

trigger_keywords: rooted_path flag meaning
when is rooted_path true vs false
absolute path indicator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: rooted_path=true when the path begins with a '/' (i.e. it is an absolute path). rooted_path=false for relative paths that do not start with '/'. For URLs with an authority component, the path is always rooted. Print as rooted_path=true or rooted_path=false.

trigger_keywords: fragment printed when read_fragment false or absent
parse_url facade optional fragment output
when is fragment_value line omitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the parse_url facade, fragment_value=<value> is only printed when read_fragment=true is explicitly provided in the input. If read_fragment is absent or false, the fragment_value line is omitted entirely. Similarly, query_values=[...] is only printed when a query_key is provided. The fragment field in the main component block is always printed regardless.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: align with however we did the encoded vs decoded representation split last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The encoded/decoded dual-representation pattern established in Feature 1: inspect_encoded_url vs inspect_decoded_url vs the parse_url facade with decoded=true/false, where userinfo format, host format, and path segment format all differ between the two representations as specified in feature1_1, feature1_2, and feature1_3 test cases

If it's still unclear, feel free to ask with more specific context about which feature or output field you're asking about.

feature1_3_parse_facade_and_lazy_decodin.description: Parse through the high-level parser, selecting decoded or encoded output and reporting deferred decoding failures as neutral errors.

feature1_4_reject_malformed_references.j.description: Reject malformed references and print a language-neutral error category plus the offending input.

feature2_1_append_path_segments.json.description: Append one or more literal path segments while preserving the existing query and fragment and escaping embedded separators.

feature2_2_replace_last_path_segment.jso.description: Replace the final path segment with a literal sibling segment while preserving query and fragment data.

feature2_3_resolve_relative_references.j.description: Resolve a relative reference against a base reference using standard dot-segment, query, fragment, and authority rules.

feature3_1_encoded_query_updates.json.description: Add, set, and remove encoded query parameters while preserving ordering and valueless parameters.

feature3_2_decoded_query_updates.json.description: Manipulate query parameters through decoded Unicode names and values while serializing them back to escaped text.

feature4_1_uri_iri_conversion.json.description: Convert reference text between URI-safe ASCII form and IRI Unicode form while preserving component meaning.

feature4_2_normalize_references.json.description: Normalize references by canonicalizing scheme and host case, removing default ports, resolving dot segments, and normalizing safe escapes.

feature5_2_register_path_only_schemes.js.description: Register a path-only scheme and render paths without an authority separator.

feature5_3_reject_invalid_scheme_registr.description: Reject inconsistent scheme registration metadata using language-neutral error output.

feature6_1_compare_reference_identity.js.description: Compare parsed references by normalized structural identity and report hash-set distinctness with their rendered text.
